meta.note: Hand-curated sample run so the demo replays without an API key. Switch to 'Run live' to generate a real one with your own model.
meta.truth_hierarchy:
Truth hierarchy (highest authority first). When sources disagree, the higher
level wins, and the conflict must still be flagged - never silently dropped:
1. CTO decision           - explicit decisions stated by the CTO
2. Written policy/contract - signed documents and published policy
3. Ops lead statement     - operational practice described by the ops lead
4. Team anecdote          - individual recollections, support stories

wiki.project_summary: FlowBook is a salon booking app. V1 covers booking with deposits, cancellation/refund handling via PayFlow, fixed 15-minute slot granularity with per-service durations, and SMS/push notifications.

claims.claim: Every booking made through the app takes a 20% deposit at booking time, with no exceptions.

sources.speaker: Mai (CTO)
sources.quote: Every booking takes a 20 percent deposit at the time of booking. No exceptions.

claims.claim: Pilot salons in practice skip deposits for walk-ins and trusted regulars.

sources.speaker: Linh (Ops Lead)
sources.quote: our pilot salons skip deposits for walk-ins and for regulars they trust

claims.claim: PayFlow is the signed payment provider for v1, supporting card and local wallets, with automatic refunds.

sources.speaker: Mai (CTO)
sources.quote: We already signed with PayFlow for v1. Card and local wallets. Refunds have to be automatic

claims.claim: Cancellation is free with full deposit refund up to 24 hours before the appointment; inside 24 hours the deposit is forfeit, split 80% salon / 20% platform.

sources.speaker: Mai (CTO)
sources.quote: Free cancellation up to 24 hours before the appointment, full deposit refund.

claims.claim: Most salon owners in practice honor cancellations up to 48 hours before the appointment.

sources.speaker: Linh (Ops Lead)
sources.quote: most owners quietly honor cancellations up to 48 hours before

claims.claim: Slot granularity is fixed at 15 minutes platform-wide; service durations are set per service by each salon.

sources.speaker: Mai (CTO)
sources.quote: slot granularity is fixed at 15 minutes platform-wide

claims.claim: Salons want per-staff service durations eventually; per-service durations are acceptable for v1.

sources.speaker: Linh (Ops Lead)
sources.quote: Salons want per-staff durations eventually ... For v1 per-service is fine.

claims.claim: V1 sends booking confirmation and a 24-hour reminder via SMS and push; email is optional and must not block v1.

sources.speaker: Mai (CTO)
sources.quote: Booking confirmation and a reminder 24 hours before. SMS plus push.

claims.claim: A customer abroad could not cancel because OTP verification required SMS to her local number.

sources.speaker: Linh (Ops Lead)
sources.quote: a lady got the reminder while abroad and couldn't cancel because the app needed an OTP via SMS

conflicts.description: The CTO mandates a 20% deposit on every app booking with no exceptions, while pilot salons in practice skip deposits for walk-ins and trusted regulars.
conflicts.resolution: Spec enforces deposits on all app bookings per W1. Flag for humans: if owners bypass the app for walk-ins/regulars, booking volume will under-report; consider a salon-side 'deposit waiver' permission as a v2 decision for the CTO.

conflicts.description: Official cancellation policy is a 24-hour free-cancellation window, but most owners in practice honor 48 hours.
conflicts.resolution: Spec implements the 24-hour window per W4, but makes the window a per-salon configurable value (default 24h) so current 48h floor practice can be represented without a code change. Confirm the configurable default with the CTO.

conflicts.notes: W6 vs W7 is not a conflict: per-staff durations are an acknowledged v2 wish, and per-service durations are accepted for v1 by both sources. W9 is an edge case input to requirements, not a contradiction.

spec.feature_name: FlowBook v1 — Booking, deposit and cancellation flow
spec.summary: Customers book salon services through FlowBook, pay a 20% deposit via PayFlow at booking time, and can cancel with automatic refunds inside a configurable free-cancellation window (default 24 hours). Slots are 15-minute granularity with per-service durations. Confirmations and reminders go out via SMS and push.
out_of_scope: Per-staff service durations (v2 candidate, per W7)
Email notifications (optional, must not block v1, per W8)
Salon-side deposit waivers for walk-ins/regulars (pending CTO decision, see conflict C1)

requirements.title: Deposit collection at booking
requirements.statement: The system collects a deposit equal to 20% of the total service price at booking time through PayFlow, for every booking created in the app.
acceptance_criteria: Given a customer confirms a booking, when payment is initiated, then PayFlow charges exactly 20% of the listed service price as a deposit.
Given the deposit charge fails, when the customer does not retry successfully, then no booking is created and the slot is released.
Given any booking channel inside the app (search, rebook, walk-in entry), when a booking is created, then a deposit is always charged.

requirements.title: Cancellation window and automatic refunds
requirements.statement: The system refunds the full deposit automatically via PayFlow when a booking is cancelled at or before the salon's free-cancellation window, which defaults to 24 hours before the appointment start.
acceptance_criteria: Given a booking is cancelled 24 hours or more before the appointment start, when the cancellation is confirmed, then PayFlow issues a full automatic refund with no manual support step.
Given a booking is cancelled inside the window, when the cancellation is confirmed, then the deposit is forfeited and split 80% to the salon and 20% to the platform.
Given a salon admin sets a custom free-cancellation window, when a customer cancels, then the salon's configured window is applied instead of the 24-hour default.

requirements.title: Slot model
requirements.statement: The system schedules appointments on a fixed 15-minute slot grid platform-wide, with each salon defining a duration per service.
acceptance_criteria: Given a salon defines a 40-minute service, when a customer books it, then the calendar blocks the next contiguous slots covering 45 minutes (rounded up to slot granularity).
Given any salon on the platform, when slots are generated, then slot boundaries fall only on 15-minute marks.

requirements.title: Booking notifications
requirements.statement: The system sends a booking confirmation immediately after deposit capture and a reminder 24 hours before the appointment, each via both SMS and push notification.
acceptance_criteria: Given a deposit is captured, when the booking is created, then the customer receives a confirmation via SMS and push within 1 minute.
Given an upcoming appointment, when the time reaches 24 hours before start, then the customer receives a reminder via SMS and push.
Given the customer's device has no push permission, when notifications are sent, then SMS is still delivered independently.

requirements.title: Cancellation must not depend on SMS OTP
requirements.statement: The system allows an authenticated customer to cancel a booking without requiring an SMS OTP to a local phone number.
acceptance_criteria: Given a logged-in customer abroad without access to local SMS, when they cancel a booking, then the cancellation completes using their existing session authentication.
Given a cancellation request, when additional verification is required, then at least one non-SMS method (e.g. email link or in-app confirmation) is available.

issues: AC3 references an in-app 'walk-in entry' channel that is not defined anywhere in the wiki; either define the channel list or drop the enumeration.
The per-salon configurable window comes from conflict resolution C2, which still needs CTO confirmation; mark R2 as blocked on that sign-off.
No upper/lower bound stated for the configurable window (could a salon set 0 hours?).
The 1-minute confirmation delivery target in AC1 is an invented number with no wiki source; confirm or remove.
R5 is derived from a single team anecdote (W9, lowest authority); acceptable as a requirement but the non-SMS verification method list should be a product decision, not inferred.

blocking_issues: R2 depends on conflict C2 resolution (configurable cancellation window) which awaits CTO confirmation.
Deposit policy conflict C1 (walk-ins/regulars) must be acknowledged by a human before engineering handoff.

turns.message: R1 AC2 releases the slot when the deposit charge fails, but nothing defines how long the slot is held while payment is pending. Without a hold timeout two customers can race for the same slot, or a slot stays locked forever behind an abandoned checkout. Also AC3 enumerates a 'walk-in entry' channel that exists nowhere in the wiki — engineering cannot build against an undefined channel.

R4 AC1 demands delivery 'within 1 minute' — an invented SLA with no wiki source and no way to verify against carrier delays. R2 lets a salon configure the cancellation window with no bounds: zero hours? a year? And the 80/20 forfeit split has no rounding rule, so odd deposit amounts are untestable to the cent.

Three concessions, one defense. R1: drop the channel enumeration from AC3 (keep 'every booking created in the app', per W1) and add a 10-minute payment hold — slot releases automatically if the deposit is not captured. R4: replace the 1-minute SLA with 'triggered immediately on the PayFlow capture webhook', which is observable. R2: bound the window to 0–168 hours, default 24, pending the CTO confirmation already flagged in C2. I defend the 80/20 split as policy (W4); rounding rule goes to QA's next point.

Accepting the 10-minute hold. New issue: R2's automatic refunds need idempotency — PayFlow webhooks can retry, and a double-fired refund is real money. The spec must state one refund per booking id, enforced server-side. And R5's session-only cancellation is fine for v1, but say explicitly that step-up verification for high-value refunds is out of scope, or someone will build it ad hoc.

The webhook trigger fixes R4 — conceded. Still open from round 1: the forfeit rounding rule on R2. Propose: platform fee rounds down to the smallest currency unit, salon receives the remainder, so the two parts always sum to the deposit exactly. That is a deterministic, testable assertion.

Agreed on all three: refund idempotency keyed on booking id goes into R2's acceptance criteria; QA's round-down rule for the platform fee is adopted as stated; and step-up verification moves explicitly to out-of-scope with v2 noted. With those amendments I consider the standing challenges closed.

rulings.rationale: Eng's race condition is real and the channel enumeration was untraced.
rulings.amendment: AC3 drops the channel enumeration; new AC: a slot is held for 10 minutes pending deposit capture and is released automatically if payment is not completed.

rulings.rationale: Window bounds, refund idempotency and the rounding rule all emerged unrebutted.
rulings.amendment: Cancellation window is configurable 0–168 hours (default 24, pending CTO sign-off per C2); exactly one automatic refund per booking id, enforced server-side against webhook retries; forfeit split rounds the platform fee down to the smallest currency unit with the salon receiving the remainder.

rulings.rationale: No party challenged the slot model in either round.
The 1-minute SLA was invented; the webhook trigger is observable and was accepted by QA.
rulings.amendment: AC1 reads: confirmation is sent immediately upon receipt of the PayFlow capture webhook, via SMS and push.

rulings.rationale: Session-auth cancellation stands; the boundary had to be made explicit.
rulings.amendment: Add to out-of-scope: step-up verification for high-value refund cancellations (v2 candidate).

outcome.summary: The debate closed one race condition, removed two invented or untraced details, and hardened refunds with idempotency and a deterministic rounding rule. Four of five requirements were amended; R3 stands as written. Conflicts C1 and C2 remain flagged for human sign-off.
